content.text: Controlling which network destinations an AI agent can reach is one of the most effective containment strategies available. An agent that cannot open arbitrary TCP connections to the internet cannot exfiltrate data, receive instructions from an attacker-controlled server, or accidentally trigger third-party webhooks. Network isolation makes the blast radius of any misbehaviour — whether caused by a prompt injection, a hallucinated tool call, or a compromised dependency — dramatically smaller and more auditable.

Network isolation is the practice of restricting an agent's ability to initiate or receive network connections to only the set of hosts, ports, and protocols it legitimately needs. Instead of running in the host network namespace where any process can open a socket to any IP address, an isolated agent runs inside a tightly controlled network environment — a container with a custom network policy, a virtual machine with restricted routing, or a process wrapped by an egress proxy that enforces an allowlist of approved destinations.

The term covers several complementary mechanisms: namespace isolation (Linux network namespaces prevent the process from seeing or using the host's network interfaces), egress filtering (an outbound proxy or firewall drops connections to unapproved destinations), DNS filtering (a local resolver that only resolves approved domain names), and ingress control (preventing external actors from opening connections into the sandbox). Together these mechanisms form a boundary between the agent's execution environment and the broader network.

Network isolation is distinct from — but complementary to — filesystem isolation and system call filtering. An agent that cannot write arbitrary files but can freely open outbound TCP connections can still exfiltrate the data it reads. Full sandboxing requires all three control planes to be active simultaneously.

Agentic systems interact with tools that fetch URLs, execute code, query databases, and send messages. Each of those tool calls is a potential network operation. Without explicit control over which network endpoints those operations can reach, the agent's footprint extends to the entire internet — and the entire internet can potentially influence or receive data from the agent.

A prompt injection in a retrieved document can instruct the agent to POST sensitive context — API keys, user records, private documents — to an attacker-controlled webhook. Without egress filtering, the tool call succeeds silently.
A compromised agent could poll a remote server for updated instructions, turning a one-time vulnerability into a persistent backdoor. Outbound connection blocking eliminates this channel.

Agents running on cloud VMs have access to the instance metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254 or fd00:ec2::254). A single HTTP request to that address can yield credentials with broad cloud permissions. Blocking access to link-local ranges prevents this.

A hallucinated tool call could trigger a real webhook, send an email via an open relay, or place an order on an external API. Allowlisting the exact set of approved endpoints makes these accidents impossible.

A compromised Python dependency the agent imports could attempt to beacon home or download additional payloads. Network isolation ensures that even a malicious library cannot establish outbound connections beyond the approved allowlist.
Even if an attacker successfully injects instructions into the agent's context, those instructions cannot reach an external server or phone home because the network boundary blocks the attempt.
Every allowed and denied connection attempt is logged at the proxy or firewall, giving a complete record of the agent's network activity for forensic review and compliance reporting.

By publishing only the set of approved endpoints, the operator reduces the number of services that need to be hardened against abuse. A service not reachable from the sandbox does not need to account for agent-initiated requests in its threat model.
When a tool is reviewed for inclusion, its network requirements must be documented and approved before the tool can function. This makes the network graph of the system explicit rather than implicit.

Network isolation is a complementary layer that remains effective even if another control (filesystem restrictions, syscall filtering) is bypassed. An attacker who gains arbitrary code execution inside the sandbox is still blocked from reaching the internet.

Every new tool or external dependency requires an explicit allowlist entry. Teams that move quickly will find the allowlist process a friction point, particularly in early development where tool requirements are still being discovered.

DNS queries are UDP datagrams that bypass many TCP-level firewalls. A sufficiently determined attacker can encode data in DNS query hostnames (DNS tunnelling) to exfiltrate information even with egress TCP blocked. DNS filtering or a local DNS resolver is required to close this channel.

An egress proxy that can only see the SNI hostname (not the full URL or body) cannot detect data exfiltration within a permitted HTTPS connection. Full TLS interception (MITM proxy) requires installing a custom CA root in the sandbox and introduces its own trust complications.

Routing all outbound traffic through an egress proxy adds a network hop. For high-frequency tool calls (embedding lookups, short LLM inference calls) the added round-trip time accumulates and can visibly degrade agent responsiveness.

Many SaaS APIs sit behind CDN or anycast networks with large, shifting IP pools. Allowlisting by IP is brittle; allowlisting by hostname (domain allowlist + DNS validation) is more robust but requires careful implementation to prevent DNS rebinding attacks.

Network isolation is implemented as a stack of complementary controls, each targeting a different layer of the networking model. A single control point is rarely sufficient; production deployments combine several layers to eliminate the bypass paths that would otherwise exist if any single mechanism were the sole defence.

The Linux kernel's network namespace mechanism gives each container or process group its own independent view of the networking stack: its own loopback interface, its own routing table, its own set of firewall rules, and optionally its own virtual Ethernet (veth) pair that connects it to the host. When a container is started without the

flag, Docker and other container runtimes automatically create a fresh network namespace, which means the container cannot see or access the host's network interfaces directly. Communication in and out of the namespace flows only through the virtual Ethernet pair, which can be firewall-ruled or routed through a proxy.

Inside a container's network namespace, or at the bridge level on the host, iptables or nftables rules govern which outbound packets are forwarded. A default-deny FORWARD rule combined with explicit ACCEPT rules for approved destination IPs or port ranges enforces an IP-level allowlist. This approach is fast (kernel-level packet inspection) but coarse: it works on IP addresses and ports, not on hostnames or URLs, so it is typically combined with a DNS allowlist to ensure that approved hostnames always resolve to the same approved IPs.
A more flexible approach routes all outbound HTTP/S traffic through a local proxy (Squid, mitmproxy, or a custom Go/Python proxy). The container's

environment variables or iptables REDIRECT rules force all HTTP/S connections through the proxy. The proxy consults a domain allowlist and rejects any request to an unapproved host, returning a 403. This approach operates at the HTTP layer and can inspect the requested URL, not just the IP:port pair, making it more accurate than pure packet filtering.

is pointed at a local DNS resolver (e.g. CoreDNS or a custom stub resolver) that only resolves names on the approved domain list and returns NXDOMAIN for everything else. This stops domain-level discovery before a TCP connection is even attempted, and also prevents DNS tunnelling by rejecting queries for arbitrary hostnames that could encode exfiltrated data in their subdomains.
objects enforce pod-level egress rules via the CNI plugin. A policy with

allowlist restricts which other pods, services, or external CIDR blocks the agent pod can reach. This provides namespace-scoped isolation that is declarative, version-controlled, and audited by the same GitOps pipeline as the rest of infrastructure.

The diagram below shows a layered network isolation architecture. The agent process and its tool runner share an isolated sandbox (a container or VM with a dedicated network namespace). All outbound TCP traffic is forced through an egress proxy or policy engine, which consults an allowlist and either forwards the connection to an approved endpoint or drops it and logs the attempt. DNS queries are handled by a local filtering resolver. A separate audit log receives a record of every connection decision.

attrs.alt: Agent network isolation architecture: sandbox, egress proxy with allowlist, allowed endpoints, blocked destinations, and audit log
attrs.title: Network Isolation Architecture

content.text: Think of the agent's sandbox as a research assistant working in a secured room. The assistant has a telephone, but it can only dial numbers that appear on an approved list pinned to the wall. Any attempt to dial an unlisted number is blocked by the switchboard operator outside the room, who also keeps a log of every call — attempted or successful. The assistant is not prevented from thinking about dialling an arbitrary number, but the physical infrastructure ensures the call never goes through.

The switchboard operator (egress proxy) is the key insight: the agent does not need to be trusted to make the right call choices, because the enforcement is external and independent of the agent's behaviour. Even if the agent is compromised or manipulated, the operator's list constrains what is reachable.

Use network isolation whenever an agent executes code or calls tools that make outbound connections, regardless of how trusted the agent model is. The isolation protects against model misbehaviour, supply chain attacks, and prompt injection simultaneously.

Use egress filtering when the agent processes untrusted external content (web pages, emails, documents uploaded by users). Prompt injection is a realistic threat whenever the agent's context window can be influenced by an adversary.

Use DNS filtering specifically when the agent uses a code-execution tool that can run arbitrary Python, JavaScript, or shell scripts. A subprocess can bypass an HTTP proxy by opening raw TCP sockets, but it cannot resolve attacker-controlled hostnames if the DNS resolver only answers approved queries.

Use Kubernetes NetworkPolicy when deploying agents in a shared cluster. Without it, a compromised agent pod can communicate freely with other pods in the cluster, potentially accessing internal APIs or databases it should not reach.

Do not skip network isolation even for internal-only agents. A model that has been fine-tuned or whose prompt has been influenced by adversarial training data could attempt exfiltration of corporate secrets; isolation limits the damage.
Consider relaxing isolation (moving to a wider allowlist) only for development sandboxes where the agent is being debugged and data sensitivity is low. Always re-tighten before moving to production.

The following steps describe a practical implementation using Docker containers with iptables egress filtering and a filtering DNS resolver. The same principles apply to Kubernetes with NetworkPolicy, to AWS Lambda with VPC configuration, or to any container platform that exposes network control.

Define the approved domain list: enumerate every hostname and port the agent's tools need. Treat this list as a code artifact — store it in version control and review changes the same way code changes are reviewed.
Start the container with a custom network namespace (the default in Docker): do not pass
. Configure the container's DNS to point at a filtering resolver you control.
Apply iptables rules on the host that drop all FORWARD traffic from the container's virtual interface except to approved IP ranges.
Run an egress proxy (Squid or a custom proxy) that consults the domain allowlist for HTTP/S traffic, providing hostname-level filtering on top of the IP-level iptables rules.
Route all container traffic through the proxy via the
environment variables, or use iptables REDIRECT to intercept port 80/443 transparently.
Configure the proxy to emit structured logs (JSON) for every connection attempt, including timestamp, destination hostname, port, bytes transferred, and policy decision (ALLOW/BLOCK).
Write an integration test that attempts to reach a non-approved endpoint from inside the sandbox and asserts a connection error. This test should run in CI to catch regressions.

# egress_proxy.py — minimal allowlist-enforcing HTTP proxy
# Run as: python egress_proxy.py --allow-list allowed_domains.txt

import argparse
import http.server
import socket
import ssl
import urllib.parse
from pathlib import Path


def load_allowlist(path: str) -> set[str]:
    """Load approved hostnames from a newline-delimited text file."""
    lines = Path(path).read_text().splitlines()
    return {line.strip().lower() for line in lines if line.strip() and not line.startswith("#")}


class EgressProxy(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    allowlist: set[str] = set()

    def _hostname_allowed(self, host: str) -> bool:
        host = host.lower().split(":")[0]  # strip port
        # Exact match
        if host in self.allowlist:
            return True
        # Wildcard match: *.example.com covers sub.example.com
        parts = host.split(".")
        for i in range(1, len(parts)):
            wildcard = "*." + ".".join(parts[i:])
            if wildcard in self.allowlist:
                return True
        return False

    def do_CONNECT(self):
        """Handle HTTPS CONNECT tunnels."""
        host, _, port = self.path.partition(":")
        if not self._hostname_allowed(host):
            self.send_error(403, f"Host {host} not in allowlist")
            self.log_message("BLOCK CONNECT %s:%s", host, port)
            return
        # Open tunnel to upstream
        try:
            remote = socket.create_connection((host, int(port or 443)), timeout=10)
        except OSError as exc:
            self.send_error(502, str(exc))
            return
        self.send_response(200, "Connection established")
        self.end_headers()
        self.log_message("ALLOW CONNECT %s:%s", host, port)
        # Relay bytes between client and upstream
        client = self.connection
        client.setblocking(False)
        remote.setblocking(False)
        import select
        while True:
            rlist, _, xlist = select.select([client, remote], [], [client, remote], 30)
            if xlist or not rlist:
                break
            for s in rlist:
                data = s.recv(4096)
                if not data:
                    return
                other = remote if s is client else client
                other.sendall(data)

    def do_GET(self):
        parsed = urllib.parse.urlparse(self.path)
        if not self._hostname_allowed(parsed.hostname or ""):
            self.send_error(403, f"Host {parsed.hostname} not in allowlist")
            self.log_message("BLOCK GET %s", self.path)
            return
        self.log_message("ALLOW GET %s", self.path)
        # ... forward the request upstream (omitted for brevity)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    ap.add_argument("--allow-list", required=True)
    ap.add_argument("--port", type=int, default=8080)
    args = ap.parse_args()
    EgressProxy.allowlist = load_allowlist(args.allow_list)
    server = http.server.HTTPServer(("127.0.0.1", args.port), EgressProxy)
    print(f"Egress proxy listening on 127.0.0.1:{args.port}")
    server.serve_forever()

# allowed_domains.txt
# LLM inference
api.anthropic.com
api.openai.com

# Object storage
*.amazonaws.com
*.googleapis.com

# Internal services
internal-vector-db.corp
internal-tool-registry.corp

# Package mirror (for code-execution tool)
pypi.internal.corp
Example 1: Docker Container with iptables Egress Rules

The following shell script starts an agent container and applies host-level iptables rules that block all outbound traffic from the container except to an internal egress proxy and to a local DNS resolver. The proxy itself enforces the domain allowlist.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

PROXY_IP="10.0.0.5"
PROXY_PORT="8080"
DNS_IP="10.0.0.6"
CONTAINER_NET="172.20.0.0/16"  # Docker bridge network CIDR for this container

# --- Start the container WITHOUT host networking ---
docker run -d \
  --name agent-sandbox \
  --network agent-net \
  --dns "$DNS_IP" \
  --env HTTP_PROXY="http://${PROXY_IP}:${PROXY_PORT}" \
  --env HTTPS_PROXY="http://${PROXY_IP}:${PROXY_PORT}" \
  --env NO_PROXY="127.0.0.1,localhost" \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  my-agent-image:latest

# --- Apply iptables rules on the host to enforce network isolation ---
# Drop all FORWARD traffic from the container network by default
iptables -I FORWARD 1 -s "$CONTAINER_NET" -j DROP

# Allow traffic from the container to the egress proxy only
iptables -I FORWARD 1 -s "$CONTAINER_NET" \
  -d "$PROXY_IP" --dport "$PROXY_PORT" -p tcp -j ACCEPT

# Allow DNS queries from the container to the filtering resolver
iptables -I FORWARD 1 -s "$CONTAINER_NET" \
  -d "$DNS_IP" -p udp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT

# Allow established/related traffic back in (response packets)
iptables -I FORWARD 1 -m state --state ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

# Block access to cloud metadata endpoint from the container
iptables -I FORWARD 1 -s "$CONTAINER_NET" \
  -d "169.254.169.254/32" -j DROP

echo "Network isolation applied for container 'agent-sandbox'"
Example 2: Kubernetes NetworkPolicy for Agent Pod
The following Kubernetes manifest creates a NetworkPolicy that restricts egress from agent pods to only the LLM inference service (an internal ClusterIP service named
) and to the DNS server on port 53. All other egress — including any external internet traffic — is dropped by the default-deny rule.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: agent-egress-policy
  namespace: agents
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: agent
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    # Allow DNS resolution via kube-dns
    - ports:
        - port: 53
          protocol: UDP
        - port: 53
          protocol: TCP

    # Allow access to the internal LLM inference service
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: llm-services
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: llm-api
      ports:
        - port: 443
          protocol: TCP

    # Allow access to internal tool registry
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: platform
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: tool-registry
      ports:
        - port: 8443
          protocol: TCP

    # Allow access to object storage via the internal egress proxy
    - to:
        - ipBlock:
            cidr: 10.0.5.10/32  # egress proxy IP
      ports:
        - port: 8080
          protocol: TCP

    # Explicitly block access to cloud metadata
    # (Note: NetworkPolicy blocks by not allowing — there is no explicit deny;
    #  the absence of an allow rule for 169.254.0.0/16 is sufficient)

Consider a customer support agent that processes uploaded PDF attachments, queries an internal knowledge base, and calls the LLM inference API to compose a reply. The agent runs in a Docker container on an EC2 instance. Without network isolation, the agent can reach any URL — including the EC2 instance metadata endpoint at 169.254.169.254, which returns temporary IAM role credentials with broad S3 and RDS permissions. A prompt injection in a malicious PDF could instruct the agent to POST those credentials to an external server.
With network isolation applied as follows, the attack fails at the network layer regardless of whether the LLM follows the injected instruction:

# sandbox_runner.py — launch agent in isolated subprocess with env-based proxy
import os
import subprocess
import sys

ALLOWED_PROXY = "http://10.0.0.5:8080"


def run_agent_task(task_json: str) -> dict:
    """
    Execute the agent in a subprocess that has HTTP_PROXY set to the
    filtering egress proxy. The OS-level iptables rules (applied at
    container start) ensure no direct outbound TCP is possible; the
    proxy enforces the domain allowlist for approved traffic.
    """
    env = {
        **os.environ,
        "HTTP_PROXY": ALLOWED_PROXY,
        "HTTPS_PROXY": ALLOWED_PROXY,
        # Prevent libraries from bypassing the proxy for internal ranges
        "NO_PROXY": "",
    }

    # Remove any credentials that should not be accessible to the agent
    for key in ["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID", "AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY", "AWS_SESSION_TOKEN"]:
        env.pop(key, None)

    result = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "agent.main", "--task", task_json],
        env=env,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        timeout=120,
    )

    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Agent exited with code {result.returncode}: {result.stderr}")

    import json
    return json.loads(result.stdout)


# Any attempt by the agent to reach 169.254.169.254 will:
# 1. Route through the egress proxy (because HTTP_PROXY is set)
# 2. Be rejected by the proxy (169.254.x.x not in allowlist)
# 3. Additionally be blocked by iptables DROP rule at the host level
# Two independent controls both prevent the attack.

The defence-in-depth principle is illustrated here: even if the process somehow bypassed the proxy configuration (e.g., by using a socket directly), the iptables DROP rule at the host level would still block the packet before it leaves the host. No single control point is sufficient, but together they form a robust barrier.
environment variables without iptables enforcement means any code that opens a raw socket (e.g., the Python
module) bypasses the proxy entirely. The kernel does not enforce environment-variable-based proxy settings — they are only respected by libraries that honour them.
to the allowlist to "simplify" access defeats the isolation goal. An agent that can reach any host in the corporate network can still access internal databases, secret stores, and admin interfaces.

Blocking TCP/UDP port 53 to external resolvers is necessary but not sufficient. If the container can reach an attacker-controlled authoritative DNS server through a permitted path, DNS queries for crafted hostnames can still tunnel data. A local filtering resolver is required.
Not testing that isolation actually blocks:
Many teams configure network isolation and never verify it works. An integration test that attempts to reach a non-approved endpoint from inside the agent process should be part of the CI pipeline.
Running the agent container with host networking means it shares the host's network namespace and can reach anything the host can. This negates container-level isolation entirely.

On AWS, GCP, and Azure, link-local addresses (169.254.169.254, fd00:ec2::254) are accessible from any EC2/GCE instance without routing through an internet gateway. An explicit DROP rule for these CIDRs must be added; they are not covered by a default-deny on 0.0.0.0/0.
Default deny, then explicitly allow: every allowlist should start with a deny-all rule. Grant access to individual hostnames and ports only after reviewing why the tool needs them.

Keep the allowlist in version control: treat the domain allowlist as infrastructure code. Review additions in pull requests, require a justification comment for each entry, and enforce this policy in CI.

Layer multiple controls: use network namespace + iptables + egress proxy + DNS filtering together. Any single control can be bypassed by a sufficiently motivated attacker or a library that opens raw sockets; the combination is much harder to circumvent.

Emit structured logs from the proxy: log every ALLOW and BLOCK decision with a timestamp, destination, bytes, and the agent task ID. Ship these logs to a SIEM. An unusual spike in BLOCK events is an early signal of a prompt injection attempt.

Alert on blocked connection attempts: a legitimate, correctly programmed agent should never attempt to connect to an unapproved host. Any BLOCK event should generate an alert for review, not be silently discarded.

Scope credentials to the minimum: even if an agent somehow accessed the cloud metadata endpoint, the damage is bounded if the instance's IAM role has only the permissions required for the agent's task (e.g., read-only access to a specific S3 bucket).
Apply host-based rules in addition to container-level rules: iptables rules applied at the host ensure that even a container escape does not grant unrestricted network access.

Network isolation is generally low-overhead at rest: network namespaces have near-zero cost when no traffic is flowing, and iptables rules are evaluated in linear order at packet-processing time in the kernel. The bottleneck is almost always the egress proxy, which introduces a small additional RTT per connection. For a typical HTTPS request to the LLM inference API (already taking 200–2000 ms for the model to respond), a proxy overhead of 1–5 ms is negligible.
High-frequency patterns that deserve attention include:

Embedding lookups: if the agent calls a vector database on each tool turn, the proxy must handle hundreds of short HTTPS requests per minute per agent. Use connection pooling (HTTP keep-alive) to amortize the cost of new TLS handshakes. A well-configured proxy should add less than 1 ms per request under these conditions.

DNS filtering overhead: a local CoreDNS instance handling thousands of queries per second per node adds negligible latency (under 1 ms per query for cached responses) and no perceptible overhead for typical agent workloads.

iptables rule table size: very large iptables rule sets (thousands of rules) degrade packet-forwarding throughput because rules are evaluated sequentially. For large allowlists, use ipsets (sets of IP addresses matched in O(1) via a hash table) instead of individual iptables -d rules.

Proxy horizontal scaling: a single egress proxy becomes a bottleneck when hundreds of agent containers are running simultaneously. Deploy the proxy as a horizontally scaled service (multiple instances behind a load balancer) and ensure the allowlist is loaded from a shared config store rather than the local filesystem.
Kernel-level FORWARD/OUTPUT rules; default-deny with IP-range allowlists; built into every Linux kernel
Mature proxy with ACL-based domain allowlists, TLS bump for HTTPS inspection, detailed access logs
Python-scriptable HTTP/S proxy; useful for development inspection and policy prototyping; not recommended for high-throughput production
Plugin-based DNS server; the forward plugin with an ACL or the acl plugin enables domain allowlisting; standard in Kubernetes
Declarative egress rules scoped to pods by label selectors; enforced by CNI plugins such as Calico, Cilium, or Weave
Layer 7 network policy including HTTP path and DNS FQDN filtering; significantly richer than standard NetworkPolicy; lower overhead than userspace proxy
Stateful (SG) and stateless (NACL) IP-level rules applied at the hypervisor; useful as an additional layer but cannot filter by hostname
Using Cilium for FQDN-Based Egress Policy

# Cilium CiliumNetworkPolicy with FQDN-based egress filtering
# Requires Cilium CNI with DNS proxy enabled
apiVersion: cilium.io/v2
kind: CiliumNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: agent-fqdn-egress
  namespace: agents
spec:
  endpointSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: agent
  egress:
    # Allow DNS (required for FQDN policy to work)
    - toEndpoints:
        - matchLabels:
            k8s:io.kubernetes.pod.namespace: kube-system
            k8s:k8s-app: kube-dns
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "53"
              protocol: UDP
          rules:
            dns:
              - matchPattern: "*"

    # Allow HTTPS to specific FQDNs only
    - toFQDNs:
        - matchName: "api.anthropic.com"
        - matchName: "api.openai.com"
        - matchPattern: "*.googleapis.com"
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "443"
              protocol: TCP

    # Allow access to internal service by label
    - toEndpoints:
        - matchLabels:
            app: internal-vector-db
      toPorts:
        - ports:
            - port: "6333"
              protocol: TCP

Network isolation is a containment control, not a prevention control. It reduces the impact of a compromise rather than preventing the compromise from occurring. This distinction matters for threat modelling: isolation is not a substitute for keeping the agent's dependencies updated, auditing tool implementations, or preventing prompt injection; it is an additional layer that limits what happens if those controls fail.

Proxy availability: the egress proxy is now in the critical path for every outbound tool call. A proxy crash or network partition between the sandbox and the proxy causes all outbound calls to fail. Run the proxy with redundancy (multiple instances) and monitor its availability with a synthetic health check.

Allowlist staleness: a hostname added to the allowlist may later point to a third-party server that has been compromised or sold to a new owner. Periodically review the allowlist and remove entries that are no longer needed. Treat the allowlist as a live document, not a set-and-forget artifact.

DNS rebinding attacks: a malicious server could return an approved IP for the initial DNS lookup (passing the allowlist check) and then change its DNS record to point to an internal IP before the TCP connection is made. Mitigate this by pinning resolved IPs at connection time or by using a proxy that re-validates the destination IP against the allowlist after DNS resolution.

HTTPS inspection and certificate trust: if the proxy performs TLS interception, the proxy's CA must be trusted by the agent's TLS stack. A bug in the proxy's certificate validation could allow a MITM attack between the sandbox and the upstream service. Use a well-audited proxy library and keep it updated.

Container escape: if an attacker achieves a container escape (e.g., via a kernel vulnerability), they land in the host network namespace where the agent's container-level isolation no longer applies. Host-level iptables rules — applied before the container starts and managed by a privileged process outside the container — provide a residual layer of protection even after an escape.
Network isolation limits the blast radius of a compromised or manipulated agent by preventing it from reaching unapproved hosts, regardless of what the model is instructed to do.
The foundational pattern is default-deny plus an explicit allowlist: block everything, then open only the specific hostnames, ports, and protocols that the agent's tools require.

Environment-variable proxy settings (HTTP_PROXY) are not sufficient on their own; they are bypassed by raw socket calls. Kernel-level enforcement via iptables or eBPF is required to cover all traffic.
DNS is a covert channel that survives TCP-level blocking. A local filtering DNS resolver that only answers approved domain queries is necessary to prevent DNS tunnelling.
Cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254) are accessible from within VMs without internet routing and must be explicitly blocked to prevent credential theft.

Layering iptables + egress proxy + DNS filtering + Kubernetes NetworkPolicy gives defence in depth: each layer independently enforces the boundary, so a bypass of one control does not grant full internet access.
Audit logging at the proxy is essential: alert on any BLOCK event, because a well-configured agent should never attempt to reach an unapproved host; a blocked attempt is a signal worth investigating.
Cilium with FQDN-based policy provides the most ergonomic and performant combination of hostname-aware filtering and kernel-level enforcement for Kubernetes deployments.

Network isolation is a containment control, not a prevention control: it reduces the damage from a successful attack, but does not replace prompt injection prevention, dependency auditing, or least-privilege credential design.
